coredata.dc:title: Bacterial cellulose nanofiber reinforced self-healing hydrogel to construct a theranostic platform of antibacterial and enhanced wound healing
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Wenping
Yu, Junjie
Li, Qingxue
Wang, Heng
Liu, Xiaoli
Li, Pingyun
Jiang, Xiaohong
Yang, Jiazhi
coredata.dc:description: In order to promote wound healing, self-healing hydrogels with moisturizing property are employed as wound dressing. In this study, bacterial cellulose nanofibers (BCN) with high mechanical strength are used as reinforcement to improve the mechanical properties of self-healing hydrogels. A multifunctional self-healing hydrogel has been constructed by incorporating natural biomass, including Ag hybrid bacterial cellulose nanofiber (Ag-BCN), resveratrol (Res), and carbon nanodots (CNDs). The results of in vitro experiments demonstrate that the mechanical strength of the hybrid hydrogel was increased by 6 times with the addition of Ag-BCN, which also offers excellent antibacterial efficiency (S. aureus 99.99 % and E. coli 99.68 %). The hydrogel with CNDs can observe the healing process of the crack in real time and realize the controlled release of Res through photothermal effect. Moreover, the results of animal model experiments indicate that the prepared hydrogel could reduce the infection of the wound, effectively shorten the progress of wound healing (from 21d to 14 d). All the results imply that the prepared hydrogel has great promise in the application of skin wound healing.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bacterial cellulose nanofiber
Hydrogel
Wound healing

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research is financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51572124), Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20210684, BK20230940, and BK20243017), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (30920130121001), and a project funded by Jiangsu Funding Program for Excellent Postdoctoral Talent.

This research is financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 51572124 ), Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province ( BK20210684 and BK20230940 ), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities ( 30920130121001 ), and a project funded by Jiangsu Funding Program for Excellent Postdoctoral Talent .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) SEM image of BCN, (b) SEM image of Ag-BCN, (c) UV/vis absorption spectra, (d) XRD patterns, and (e) XPS spectra of BCN and Ag-BCN; (f) High-resolution spectrum of Ag3d.
SEM images of S-gel (a) and FS-gel (b), (c) FTIR spectra of S-gel and FS-gel, (d) stress-strain curve (n = 3), (e) schematic illustration and microscopic structure of FS-gel.

(a) The self-healing capability: (a1, a2) love heart-shaped FS-gel (a2 is stained by Congo red), (a3) two halves of love heart-shaped FS-gel, (a4) the self-healed heart-shaped hydrogel composed of two halves of different hydrogels, (a5) lifting and (a6) stretching the self-healed hydrogel, (b-e) fluorescent images of self-healing process of hydrogel.

(a) Antibacterial activity against S. aureus and E. coli, (b, d) The corresponding statistical histogram of S. aureus colonies (b) and E. coli colonies (d), (n = 3) *indicates the significance comparison between the experimental group and group of S-gel, (***p < 0.001), (c, e) antimicrobial durability performed on FS-gel in S. aureus (c) and E. coli (e), (inset: zone of inhibition of FS-gel at day 10, n = 3).

(a) DPPH solution fade by FS-gel with different concentration of Res, (b) the antioxidant capacity of FS-gel with different concentrations of Res (*p < 0.05, ***p < 0.001), (c) antioxidant efficiency ratio (*** p < 0.001), (d) thermal images of S-gel and FS-gel, (e) photothermal curve of FS-gel. (Inset: photothermal stability with 3 cycles of on/off laser (1.0 W·cm−2)), (f) cumulative drug release curve (inset: release behavior of Res without laser irradiation). (n = 3).

(a) Cell viability assays. Incubation time: 24 h and 48 h. Error bar: standard deviation (n = 5); fluorescent images stained by Calcein-AM/PI of cells incubated with S-gel (b), FS-gel (c) and FS-gel+Laser (d). (n = 3, **p < 0.01).

(a) Schematic diagram of wound and treatment, (b) wounds on day1, 7, 14, and 21 treated with S-gel/CNDs, S-gel/CNDs/Ag-BCN, and FS-gel, (c) schematic diagrams of wound contraction, (d) statistically calculated wound healing rate. (n = 5).
Wound sections stained by H&E on day 7, 14, and 21.
Promoting the healing of an infected wound using FS-gel (an antibacterial ROS-scavenging hydrogel).

head.ce:title: Bacterial cellulose nanofiber reinforced self-healing hydrogel to construct a theranostic platform of antibacterial and enhanced wound healing

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, Nanjing University of Science and Technology, 200 Xiao Ling Wei Street, Nanjing, Jiangsu Province 210094, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Nanjing University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Engineering, Nanjing University of Science and Technology, 200 Xiao Ling Wei Street, Nanjing, Jiangsu Province, 210094, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Pharmacology and Safety Evaluation of Chinese Materia Medica, Department of Pharmacy, Nanjing University of Chinese Medicine, 138 Xianlin Avenue, Qixia District, Nanjing, Jiangsu Province 210023, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Pharmacology and Safety Evaluation of Chinese Materia Medica

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Pharmacology and Safety Evaluation of Chinese Materia Medica, Department of Pharmacy, Nanjing University of Chinese Medicine, 138 Xianlin Avenue, Qixia District, Nanjing, Jiangsu Province, 210023, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In order to promote wound healing, self-healing hydrogels with moisturizing property are employed as wound dressing. In this study, bacterial cellulose nanofibers (BCN) with high mechanical strength are used as reinforcement to improve the mechanical properties of self-healing hydrogels. A multifunctional self-healing hydrogel has been constructed by incorporating natural biomass, including Ag hybrid bacterial cellulose nanofiber (Ag-BCN), resveratrol (Res), and carbon nanodots (CNDs). The results of in vitro experiments demonstrate that the mechanical strength of the hybrid hydrogel was increased by 6 times with the addition of Ag-BCN, which also offers excellent antibacterial efficiency (S. aureus 99.99 % and E. coli 99.68 %). The hydrogel with CNDs can observe the healing process of the crack in real time and realize the controlled release of Res through photothermal effect. Moreover, the results of animal model experiments indicate that the prepared hydrogel could reduce the infection of the wound, effectively shorten the progress of wound healing (from 21d to 14 d). All the results imply that the prepared hydrogel has great promise in the application of skin wound healing.

ce:section.ce:para: Skin is an important part of human body, which can not only protect the inner organs against external damage, but also serve multiple functions, such as regulating body temperature, inhibiting fluid loss and resisting microbial invasion [1–5]. However, due to the characteristics of large direct contact area with the outside world, skin is frequently injured by various physical damages and diseases, such as temperature fluctuations, pressure, chemicals, and invasion of pathogens [6–8]. Once the skin is damaged, the healing process would be delayed by interaction of endogenous (oxidative stress) and exogenous factors (microbial infection) [9–12]. Therefore, it is necessary to cover the wound with functional dressings with antibacterial activity and antioxidative property, which is facilitated to promote wound healing and reduce economic losses and pain of patients [13,14].

Recently, researchers have devoted extensive efforts into the development of functional wound dressing, including emipermeable membranes, semipermeable foams, hydrocolloids, and hydrogels, to satisfy the demand of complex wound microenvironment [15,16]. Among them, hydrogels have emerged as a competitive candidate for wound healing due to its capacity to sustain an ideal moist environment for healing, outstanding tissue exudates absorption, efficient delivery [17–20]. But traditional hydrogel is inevitably troubled by micro-cracks or larger splits in the progress of application, which results in the exposure of wound and intrusion of external bacteria [21,22]. In order to address this problem, self-healing hydrogel emerged as requirement. Especially, self-healing hydrogel prepared by natural biomass have become a research hotspot due to excellent biocompatibility, biodegradability, and cellular affinity [23]. However, the self-healing hydrogel prepared by natural biomass often lack adequate mechanical properties and antibacterial activity, which impedes its commercial application [24]. Some studies propose approaches to this problem, such as grafting, cross-linking, and adding one-dimensional materials (polymer fiber, bioglass fiber, and carbon nanotube etc.), which can dramatically enhance the mechanical strength of self-healing hydrogel [25–27]. But chemical modification or addition of synthetic materials result in a decrease in biocompatibility and biodegradability. Therefore, it still remains a challenge to use all-natural materials to construct a self-healing hydrogel dressing with antibacterial and antioxidant properties.

Among the natural materials, BCN prepared by microbial fermentation is a promising biomaterial, which offer huge advantages, such as excellent biocompatibility, biodegradability, and mechanical strength [28,29]. Moreover, Yang et al. deposited silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) on the surface of BCN to prepare silver-hybridized BCN, exhibiting promising potential as antibacterial agents in a range of applications [30]. At present, there are few research studies focus on adding silver hybrid BCN into self-healing hydrogels to enhance the mechanical strength and antibacterial properties. As for antioxidant property, Res has attracted much attention due to the acceptable reducibility effects [31]. Res, which is extracted from natural plant (such as grapes, cranberries, and peanuts), has been commonly used in scavenging free radical in human body to delay senescence [32]. Meanwhile, Res also offers huge advantages in anti-inflammatory and antibacterial activity [33]. The combined use of Res and antibacterial agents can greatly improve the antibacterial ability. Currently, it is rarely to construct self-healing hydrogel with antibacterial activity and antioxidative property for wound healing by incorporating Res and Ag-BCN.

In recent years, nanoparticles with photothermal effect, such as gold nanoparticles, black phosphorus quantum dots, polydopamine nanoparticles, and carbon nanodots have been widely used to combine with hydrogel for wound healing [34–37]. On the previous research foundation about natural biomass (including Ag-BCN, Res, and CNDs), a smart wound healing platform is designed through the selection of natural substrate chitosan derivatives [30,31,38]. The constructed wound healing platform realizes the controlled release of drugs by photothermal effect. And the excellent antibacterial activity, brilliant antioxidative property of the wound healing platform effectively shorten the wound healing progress of SD rats (from 21d to 14 d). This work gives a new way to develop novel self-healing hydrogel for promoting wound healing (Scheme 1 ).

Polyethylene glycol (PEG) was purchased from Tianjin Sciens Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. Carboxymethyl chitosan (CMCS) was purchased from Nanjing Juyou Scientific Equipment Co. Ltd. Resveratrol (Res) was procured from Nanjing Xianfeng Nanotechnology Co., Ltd. CNDs nanoparticle was prepared by improving the previous work [38].

BCN is prepared by improving the previous work [30]. The prepared BC was cut into small pieces and centrifuged at a speed of 10,000 r min−1 for 30 min in a tissue homogenizer. Then the homogenate was put into a three-mouth bottle with 80 % (V/V) sulfuric acid at constant temperature water bath accompanied by intense agitation. Then, BCN suspension was obtained after purification process. Subsequently, the BCN suspension was taken out for high-speed centrifugation (10,000 r·min−1, 20 min) and dehydrated. Then, AgNO3 solution was added and soaked at 40 °C for 12 h. Then hydrazine hydrate solution was added for reduction and stirred at 45 °C for 6 h. Eventually, the Ag-BCN was obtained for the further experiment.

Difunctionalized PEG (DF-PEG) was prepared following the previous work [39]. 3 % (w/w) CMCS solution was obtained by dissolving CMCS in 2.1 % (w/w) acetic acid solution. 20 % (w/w) DF-PEG solution was prepared by dissolving DF-PEG in water. 0.25 mL DF-PEG solution was added into 0.7 g CMCS solution. The hydrogel was obtained after 30–40 s of vortex and denoted as CMCS/DF-PEG (S-gel). During the preparation of DF-PEG solution (25 mL), Ag-BCN (2 mg), Res (2 mg), and CNDs (1 mg) was added and the correspondingly obtained hydrogel was denoted as S-gel/CNDs/Ag-BCN/Res (FS-gel). In addition, the hydrogel incorporated with only CNDs was denoted as S-gel/CNDs. The hydrogel incorporated with CNDs and Ag-BCN was denoted as S-gel/CNDs/Ag-BCN.

The morphology images were collected by using scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Quant 250FEG, FEI Company, America). The Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were acquired with an ultraviolet visible spectrophotometer EVOLUTION 220 (Thermo, America). X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were recorded on a Bruker D8 Advanced Xray diffractometer (Cu Kα radiation) from German. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data was collected using a PHI Quanter II X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Ulvac-Phi, Japan) equipped with an Mg Kα excitation source. Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR) spectra were collected using a NICOLETIS10 FT-IR spectrophotometer (Thermo, America) in a spectral region between 4000 and 400 cm−1. The swelling rate of the hydrogels was detected by gravimetric method. The dry hydrogels were immersed in PBS (pH 7.4) for 24 h at room temperature. The hydrogels were taken out and weighed. The swelling ratio was defined as the weight ratio of the swollen hydrogel to the initial hydrogel. The tensile strength was measured by using a Universal Material Testing machine (Shimazu AGS-100NX, Japan) at room temperature. The hydrogels were stretched at a constant rate of 50 mm·min−1 and all samples are tested for 3 times.

One piece of FS-gel was stained by Congo red. And both of the unstained FS-gel and stained FS-gel with love heart shape are cut from the middle. And then the two halves of different hydrogels were patched into a new love heart. The two hydrogels contacted can recombine into an integrated new heart-shaped hydrogel. The healed hydrogel was stretched by the tweezers until crack. The confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM, Leica TCS SP8, Germany) was employed to further visualize the self-healing process of the S-gel and FS-gel.

The antibacterial activity was tested by plate counting method against Escherichia coli (E. coli Atcc 25922) and Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus Atcc 12600). Samples (1.5 cm) were added in a 24-well plate. 100 μL of bacterial (S. aureus and E. coli) suspension (2 × 107 CFU·mL−1) diluted with culture medium is added in each well and incubated in Luria-Bertani (LB) medium at 37 °C for 24 h. Subsequently, dilutions of the bacterial suspension were evenly spread onto LB agar plate. After incubation at 37 °C for 24 h, the bacterial colonies in plates were photographed and the corresponding bacterial number was counted. The relative bacterial inhibition ratio (%) was calculated based on the following equation:
Bacterial inhibition
%
=
C 0
− C
C 0
∗ 100 %
where C is the average colony forming units (CFUs) of samples, and C 0 is the average CFUs of controls.

In addition, long-term antimicrobial performance of FS-gel was investigated by modified Kirby–Bauer disc diffusion technique. Briefly, FS-gel with a diameter of 8 mm was placed on LB agar plate which had been previously spread with 100 μL of bacterial suspension with a concentration of 108 CFU mL−1. After incubation at 37 °C for desired time, the inhibition zone was observed and record.
All the antibacterial activity was tested for three times.

The antioxidant property of FS-gel was evaluated by 2,2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH) free radical scavenging assay. FS-gel with different contents of Res was pipetted into DPPH solution. The change on the color of DPPH solution was recorded. And the absorbance of DPPH solution at 517 nm was collected by using a Thermo NICOLETIS10 FT-IR spectrophotometer. The corresponding date before adding Res was defined as A 0, and the date after is A 1. The corresponding antioxidant efficiency ratio was calculated and normalized. The antioxidant efficiency ratio (R) is calculated based on the following equation:
R =
A 0
−
A 1
A 0
∗ 100 %
The antioxidant property was tested for three times.

The hydrogels (d = 10 mm) were irradiated with an 808 nm laser (1 W·cm−2). And the corresponding temperatures were recorded with a thermal image equipment (Gaode Zhigan Technology Co., Ltd., China). Res was chosen as a model drug to investigate the drug-controlled release properties of the FS-gel. For the drug release experiment, 20 mg FS-gel was immerged in 50 mL PBS for 80 min with or without irradiation by 808 nm laser. In this progress, the laser was turn on during the following intervals: 0–10 min, 20–30 min, 40–50 min, and 60–70 min. And the laser was turn off at 10–20 min, 30–40 min, 50–60 min, and 70–80 min. At certain time intervals, the release of Res was measured by high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC, Agilent 1200, America) at 306 nm.

S-gel (r = 6 mm) and FS-gel (r = 6 mm) were placed in the 96-well plate, and the surface of the hydrogels should be ensured to be smooth and sterilize for backup. The cytotoxicity of S-gel and FS-gel was examined on pulmonary alveolar epithelial cells (PAEpiCs) by using the standard Cell Tilter-Glo (CTG) luminescent cell viability assay. The cytotoxicity assay was tested for three times.

The Calcein-AM/propidium iodide (Calcein-AM/PI) double stain kit obtained from Yeasen Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China) was utilized to further evaluate the toxicity of S-gel and FS-gel to cells. Cells were incubated with S-gel (r = 1 cm) and FS-gel (r = 1 cm) for 12 h. The group of FS-gel+Laser was subsequently exposed to 808 nm laser (W·cm−2) for 5 min. And cells were followed by staining with calcein-AM/PI (100 μL) for 30 min and were observed and photographed using an inverted fluorescence microscope.

All animal experiments were reviewed and approved by the Animal Care and Use Committee of Nanjing University of Chinese Medicine (Protocol no. 202108A036). The effect of hydrogels on wound healing was evaluated on male Sprague-Dawley (SD) rats weighing 180–220 g. The rats were divided into three groups (n = 5 per group). And the dorsal area of all rats was depilated after anesthetizing. Subsequently, traumatic wounds were created by the surgical procedure on the back of the rats by using surgical scissors. The wounds on the rats of three groups were treated by using different hydrogels (S-gel/CNDs, S-gel/CNDs/Ag-BCN, and S-gel/CNDs/Ag-BCN/Res, respectively) with a diameter of 2 cm. Simultaneously, the wound healing progress of the rats were monitored. After 7, 14, and 21 d of treatment, the rats were euthanized, and the wound skin tissues are stained by Hematoxylin-eosin (H&E) for histological assessment.

All the data were analyzed by using Microsoft Excel 2019, and the corresponding data were plotted by using Origin Pro 2023 software (Origin Lab, USA). And the experimental results were presented as mean ± standard deviation (SD). The statistical significance was evaluated by using GraphPad Software (Prism 7.0, USA) with one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA). The statistical significance was defined as: ns > 0.05, *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001.

The SEM image of BCN was presented in Fig. 1(a). BCN presented a well-organized 3D network structure. In Fig. 1(b), BCN was coated by the Ag nanoparticles uniformly, providing good evidence of successful loading of Ag nanoparticles on the surface of BCN.

The UV–vis absorption spectra of BCN and Ag-BCN were recorded in Fig. 1(c). The absorption peak observed at 263 nm in BCN might be due to the carboxyl groups generated by the oxidation of cellulose during the process of acid hydrolysis of cellulose [40]. Silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) had strong surface plasmon resonance (SPR) effect, and the location of peak depended on the size, shape, and composition of the nanoparticles [41,42]. Therefore, morphology information of AgNPs could be obtained through analyzing the position and shift of the SPR peak. As shown in Fig. 1(c), a significant absorption peak could be observed around 410 nm in Ag-BCN, which was similar to the monodisperse AgNPs near 400 nm [41,43]. The result indicated that BCN as carrier could effectively prevent the agglomeration of AgNPs, which was consistent with the SEM image. In the meantime, the absorption peak around 263 nm disappeared due to reduction reaction by reducing agent in the process of synthesis of Ag nanoparticles. The XRD pattern of BCN and Ag-BCN were exhibited in Fig. 1(d) to further confirm the successful preparation of Ag nanoparticles on BCN. The diffraction peaks at 14.3° and 22.6° were assigned to the diffraction planes of (101) and (002) of BCN. It was noteworthy that additional peaks appeared at 38°, 44°, 64°and 77° were shown in the diffraction patterns of Ag-BCN, corresponding to the characteristic peaks of Ag (111), Ag (200), Ag (220), and Ag (311), respectively. This result further demonstrated the perfective loading of Ag nanoparticles on BCN. The XPS measurement was employed to further confirm the elemental composition of BCN and Ag-BCN. As shown in Fig. 1(e), full-range XPS spectrum of the BCN exhibited two peaks at around 539 eV and 280 eV, corresponding to O1s and C1s, respectively. In comparison, an excess signal was located at around 363 eV in the full-range XPS spectrum of Ag-BCN, which was assigned to the peak of Ag3d. The high-resolution spectrum of Ag3d (Fig. 1f) could be divided into two peaks at 371.5 eV and 365.5 eV, ascribed to Ag3d3/2 and Ag3d5/2, respectively. The corresponding binding energy difference was calculated to be 6 eV, which further indicated the zero-valent Ag exists. All the results above provided strong evidence to support the successful preparation of Ag nanoparticles on BCN [44,45].

The internal microstructure of S-gel and FS-gel was investigated by SEM, and the SEM images of S-gel and FS-gel was presented in Fig. 2(a) and (b), respectively. There were many smooth folds with certain pore structure in the SEM images of S-gel and FS-gel. Furthermore, the results showed that the morphology of FS-gel did not change significantly with the incorporation of Ag-BCN, Res, and CNDs. FTIR spectra of S-gel and FS-gel were shown in Fig. 2(c). The broad and intense peak centered at 3350 cm−1 was related to the OH stretching vibrations. The absorption peaks near 2885 cm−1 and 1720 cm−1 were associated with the stretching vibration of CH and CO, respectively. Other characteristic peaks between 1300–1000 cm−1 were attributed to CO. The enhanced absorption peak of OH (3350 cm−1) in FS-gel was due to the incorporation of Ag-BCN and Res.

The swelling rate of S-gel and FS-gel were 350 % and 230 %, respectively, suggesting a strong capacity to absorb inflammatory secretions from the wound, retain wound exudate, and maintain an ideal moist environment for healing [46–49]. But exorbitant swelling rate was often accompanied by poor mechanical properties. As shown in Fig. 2(d), the tensile strength of S-gel was only 0.18 MPa. After fiber reinforcement, the tensile strength of FS-gel was raised to 1.29 MPa, approximately 6 times higher than that of S-gel. One reason for the enhancement of mechanical property was the assembly (entanglement) of BCN with three-dimensional (3D) cross-linked network of hydrogel, which allow for advanced interactions between adjacent BCN and hydrogel [50]. In addition, intermolecular hydrogen bonds were generated between BCN and hydrogel [51]. The interpenetrating networks and abundant hydrogen bond were beneficial for the mechanical strength of fiber reinforced hydrogel (Fig. 2(e)). However, the intense hydrogen bond and crystalline region of BCN restrained the movement of molecular chains, resulted in the rigid characteristic of BCN [52]. Therefore, FS-gel suffered a decrease on the elongation at break because of the addition of BCN.

The self-healing capability of hydrogel was of vital importance to avoid the micro-cracks or larger splits generated in the progress of using, which might result in the microbial invasion. As shown in Fig. 3 , the self-healing performance of FS-gel was evaluated. One piece of heart-shaped hydrogel was stained by Congo red (Fig. 3a2) and the other one was unstained (Fig. 3a1). Both of the unstained heart-shaped hydrogel and stained heart-shaped hydrogel were cut from the middle. Then the two pieces of different hydrogels were patched into an integrated one (Fig. 3a3-a4). As shown in Fig. 3(a5), the patchwork could withstand the stretch by tweezers to a certain extent, exhibiting excellent self-healing performance. Subsequently, the stretched strength was increased until crack. As shown in Fig. 3(a6), the re-formed hydrogel cracked at the other position rather than healing site when it was stretched at both ends, implying that healed site was not the weakest point of hydrogel. The above result preliminarily proved that the FS-gel possessed excellent self-healing ability from a macroscopic perspective.

Generally, the disappearance of crack could be observed in optical microscope to further verify the self-healing property of FS-gel [53]. The addition of CNDs in FS-gel made it possible to reveal the diffusion and transfer of substances in the self-healing process from a microscopic perspective by fluorescence (FL) imaging. The CLSM images were collected to visualize the self-healing process of hydrogel. Two halves of hydrogels (S-gel and FS-gel) physically contacted to recombine into an integrated hydrogel and the local images at different time were collected (Fig. 3b-e). As shown in Fig. 3(b), there was an obvious crack between the two hydrogels in the fluorescent bright field microscopy images. In the dark field, the side of S-gel had no fluorescent signal and FS-gel showed bright signal at the excited wavelength of 405 nm. The crack shrank gradually until disappear over time (Fig. 3c-e). After the completely healing of the two kinds of hydrogels, the bright blue signal spread to the side of S-gel, indicating the diffusion and transfer of CNDs from FS-gel to S-gel. The incorporation of CNDs in the hydrogel could realize the real-time monitoring of the flaw and successfully constructed the theranostics platform on wound healing. The employment of theranostics in wound healing enabled the abilities to visualize and monitor the diseased tissues, efficient delivery kinetics, precise treatment, and simultaneously provided the possibility to tune the therapeutic strategy in a controlled and rational manner. However, the mechanical properties of the FS-gel after self-healing (H-FS-gel) exhibited in Fig. 2(d) showed an obvious decline. The relatively big and rigid BCN made it difficult to diffusion and transfer between the interface. Therefore, the mechanical properties of the H-FS-gel cannot regain its previous strength after self-healing.

The antibacterial activity of the hydrogel was particularly necessary for the application on wound dressings. The antimicrobial characteristics of S-gel, S-gel/CNDs, S-gel/Res, and FS-gel against S. aureus and E. coli were investigated to explore the effect of CNDs, Res and Ag-BCN on the antibacterial activity of hydrogel. As shown in Fig. 4(a), the S-gel and S-gel/CNDs exhibit no obvious antibacterial activity compared with the blank group. Generally, CNDs were reported to exhibit huge advantages on antibacterial treatment [54]. Currently, there were two most common antibacterial mechanisms of CNDs, namely, breaking down the membrane integrity of bacteria by attaching to bacteria, or causing oxidative damage by catalyzing oxygen to generate reactive oxygen species (ROS) [55]. In this regard, the negligible antibacterial performance of hydrogels incorporated with CNDs might be due to two reasons. The CNDs were dispersed into the self-healing hydrogel. In the process of antibacterial experiment, the encapsulation of the hydrogel to CNDs made it difficult to diffuse into the antibacterial system and contact with bacteria, resulting in low antibacterial property. At the same time, the hydrogel also limited the contact between oxygen and CNDs, resulting in a decrease in the efficiency of CNDs to catalyze oxygen to generate ROS. Hence, it was understandable that the antibacterial ability of CNDs was not revealed due to the encapsulation of hydrogels. In order to verify the above speculation and explore the photothermal effect on the antibacterial properties, the group of S-gel/CNDs was further treated with 808 nm laser. The result demonstrated that S-gel/CNDs with laser possessed non-negligible antimicrobial activity. Both of the antibacterial rates of S-gel/CNDs+Laser against S. aureus and E. coli were approximately 70 %. Photothermal effect enhanced the migration ability of CNDs in hydrogels and increased the exposure of CNDs, boosting the ability of CNDs to breaking down the bacterial membrane or generate ROS. Meanwhile, an evident reduction of S. aureus and E. coli colony numbers could be observed in the S-gel/Res treated group compared to the control group, indicating that Res could offer prominent antimicrobial activity to the hydrogel. As for the FS-gel treated group, there were only a few bacteria here and the calculated antibacterial rates of the FS-gel against S. aureus and E. coli were 99.99 % and 99.68 %, respectively. The strong antimicrobial activity of FS-gel was attributed to the combined effect of Res and Ag-BCN. In addition, long-term antimicrobial performance of FS-gel was investigated. As shown in Fig. 4(b-c), FS-gel was retained its antimicrobial activity against S. aureus (Fig. 4b) and E. coli (Fig. 4c) without obvious loss for an extended period of 10 days. The superior antibacterial activity of FS-gel made it competent material for wound treatment.

Oxidative stress caused by excessive ROS impeded the progress of wound healing. Hence, the antioxidant property of substrate material for wound treatment was of vital importance in the improvement of wound healing. Res is reported to have great potential in antioxidant treatment [31]. The antioxidant property of FS-gel contained Res was evaluated by DPPH free radical scavenging assay. As shown in Fig. 5(a), the dark ethanol solution of DPPH turned into pale yellow after addition with FS-gel. With the concentration of Res incorporated in FS-gel increased, the corresponding absorbance at 517 nm showed an obvious decline. The corresponding antioxidant capacity was calculated (Fig. 5b). The IC50 value of FS-gel for DPPH radical scavenging activity was lower than 20 μg·mL−1, indicating the strong antioxidant property of FS-gel. Furthermore, the normalized antioxidant efficiency ratio was collected to analyze the effective utilization rate of Res (Fig. 5c). 10 μg·mL−1 was determined as the optimal concentration by comprehensive consideration of both free radical scavenging effect and effective utilization rate of Res. The superior antioxidant capacity of FS-gel could solve the problem of oxidative stress in the progress of wound healing.

The incorporation of CNDs in hydrogel could not only offer the visualize and monitor performance, but also realize the controlled release of Res through photothermal effect. Owing to the excellent photothermal characteristics of the CNDs, the FS-gel was expected to exhibit prominent photothermal performance. Under 808 nm NIR light irradiation, S-gel showed no obvious temperature increase (Fig. 5d). In contrast, the temperature of FS-gel could be over 50 °C after irradiation for 9 min. The corresponding photothermal curve with irradiation time was shown in Fig. 5(e). As expected, the temperature of FS-gel increased rapidly at the beginning (24.6 to 53.3 °C) and then started to level off after 10 min, demonstrating high-efficiency photothermal conversion ability of FS-gel. Furthermore, the photothermal performance of FS-gel exhibited no obvious significantly decay during three on-off cycles of laser, indicating the brilliant photothermal stability of FS-gel. The unique photothermal property of FS-gel would offer huge advantage of laser-induced controlled drug release [56]. Primarily, the release behavior of Res without laser irradiation was exhibited in the inset of Fig. 5(f). The total release amount after extension of time could achieve 60.39 % (pH 5.5) and 54.74 % (pH 7.3). The obvious difference might be attributed to the breakage of the dynamic Schiff base linkage in the acidic environment [57]. Irradiation dependent Res cumulative release curves (Fig. 5(f)) with same release condition were recorded to explore the controlled drug release capacity of FS-gel. As a distinctive comparison, the drug release rates under irradiation of FS-gel were all higher than those without irradiation. The release rate was dramatically improved with laser irradiation and the total release amount with laser irradiation was 65.63 % (pH 5.5) and 59.94 % (pH 7.3). The above results demonstrated the superior photothermal performance of FS-gel, which was excellent enough to meet the demand on drug delivery of wound dressing.

The cytotoxicity of S-gel and FS-gel was examined on PAEpiCs by using the standard CTG luminescent cell viability assay. Cell cultures were treated with S-gel and FS-gel in the 96-well plates. As shown in Fig. 6(a), PAEpiCs maintained >85 % cell viability after 48 h incubation, indicating the excellent cell compatibility of FS-gel. In view of the safety threshold of 40 °C for cells viability, the PAEpiCs treated with FS-gel were irradiated with 808 nm laser (1 W·cm−2) for 5 min to explore the photothermal effects on cell viability [58]. Notably, the cell viability exhibited an obvious decline. Similarly, the cells incubated with S-gel and FS-gel for 24 h were stained by Calcein-AM/PI. As shown in Fig. 6(b-c), there were rarely dead cells (red) appearing in the field of vision, suggesting the low cytotoxicity of S-gel and FS-gel. Remarkably, more dead cells could be observed in the group of FS-gel+Laser (Fig. 6(d)) due to the photothermal ablation effect of FS-gel under laser irradiation. The above observations proved that FS-gel had great potential in wound healing.

To evaluate the therapeutic effect of FS-gels on wound healing, skin wounds were created on the backs of SD rats and followed by the treatment of S-gel/CNDs, S-gel/CNDs/Ag-BCN, and FS-gel, respectively (Fig. 7(a)). And the photographs and schematic diagrams of wound contraction on day 1, 7, 14, and 21 were displayed in Fig. 7(b) and (c). The corresponding statistically calculated wound healing rate was presented in Fig. 7(d). With treatment time extending, all the groups showed wound area reduction to some extent. Among all groups, FS-gel exhibited the comparatively higher promotion effect on wound healing. In order to further evaluate the progress of wound healing, the histomorphology analysis was performed by using H&E staining. As presented in Fig. 8 , there were still inflammatory cell infiltration around the wound treated with S-gel/CNDs on day 14 compared to S-gel/CNDs/Ag-BCN and FS-gel, indicating that the multifunctional hydrogels incorporated with Ag-BCN are beneficial for reducing inflammatory response. Furthermore, there were hair follicle and blood vessel neogenesis appeared successively in the groups treated with S-gel/CNDs/Ag-BCN and FS-gel on day 14 and 21, illustrating the regeneration of new skin. But no obvious skin appendages could be observed to regenerate in the group treated with S-gel/CNDs. In addition, new epidermis regenerated at the wound site for the groups treated with FS-gel, implying that the hydrogel introduced with Res was facilitate to promote epidermal remodeling. All the above results proves that FS-gel could enhanced the processing of wound healing, which was attributable to the combined advantages of a suitable moist environment provided by the multifunctional hydrogel, the good antimicrobial activity from Ag-BCN and the antioxidation property from Res. The capacity for significant acceleration on the progress of wound healing made the FS-gel a superior candidate for wound dressing in the field of tissue engineering.

In this research, natural biomass including Ag-BCN, Res, CNDs, and chitosan derivatives was used to construct a multi-functional platform. The dual-functional Ag-BCN offered FS-gel enhanced mechanical strength (6 times higher than that of S-gel) and excellent antibacterial efficiency (S. aureus 99.99 % and E. coli 99.68 %). The multi-functional CNDs introduced in FS-gel enabled the monitor of the progress of self-healing and controlled release of drug by photothermal effect. The SD rat wound model was used to evaluate the performance of the functional platform. And the results indicated that the interaction between Res and Ag-BCN reduced wound infection, weakened adverse effects of ROS, and promoted wound healing (shorten the progress of wound healing from 21d to 14 d). Although the multi-functional platform has great potential in clinical applications such as trauma treatment, space-occupying filling after tumor resection, and tissue regeneration. There were still numerous researches to be delved to realize the practical clinical translation, such as in vitro and in vivo degradation experiments and human clinical experiments.

Wenping Li: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Data curation. Junjie Yu: Data curation. Qingxue Li: Data curation. Heng Wang: Investigation. Xiaoli Liu: Writing – review & editing, Resources. Pingyun Li: Writing – review & editing. Xiaohong Jiang: Writing – review & editing. Jiazhi Yang: Writing – review & editing, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There is no declaration of interest statement.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research is financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51572124), Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20210684, BK20230940, and BK20243017), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (30920130121001), and a project funded by Jiangsu Funding Program for Excellent Postdoctoral Talent.
